articles.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists report how the body’s cold sensor TRPM8 detects both cold temperatures and menthol. The finding explains at a molecular level why mint produces a cooling sensation. The work links the same microscopic sensor to responses from both environmental cold and chemical cooling. The researchers say the discovery could inspire treatments for pain and eye disorders.

articles.title: Scientists discover the protein that malaria parasites can’t live without
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified a malaria-parasite protein called Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) as a critical vulnerability. The study describes ARK1 as acting like a traffic controller during the parasite’s unusual cell division. Because the parasite cannot live without this protein, it represents a potential target for new treatments. The report frames the finding as opening the door to antimalarial drug development.

articles.title: Scientists discover a bacterial kill switch and it could change the fight against superbugs
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists looking for new antibiotic targets report that multiple unrelated viruses can disable a bacterial protein called MurJ. MurJ is described as essential for building the bacterial cell wall. The work suggests that blocking MurJ functions like a kill switch for bacteria. The finding is presented as a potentially important avenue in efforts to combat drug-resistant infections.

articles.title: A 4,000-year-old sheep reveals the secret of an ancient plague
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists report a clue about an ancient plague that spread across Eurasia long before the Black Death. By analyzing ancient DNA, they detected Yersinia pestis in a 4,000-year-old domesticated sheep. The sheep came from a Bronze Age settlement in the Ural Mountains. The discovery is presented as evidence helping explain early forms of plague transmission and history.

articles.title: Shingles vaccine may slow biological aging and reduce inflammation
articles.summary_4_sentences: A national study examined whether shingles vaccination is associated with measures of biological aging. The analysis included more than 3,800 Americans age 70 and older. Those who received the shingles vaccine showed slower biological aging in the reported results. The summary also links vaccination with reduced inflammation.
